name: Mathematics-II
description: Discrete mathematics, transforms, and basic graph theory for IT applications.

theory: Propositional logic provides the language for specifying conditions and reasoning about program correctness.
Proof techniques such as induction and contradiction are used to verify algorithmic properties and correctness claims.
Logical equivalences simplify complex boolean expressions often found in conditionals and circuit design.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: A proposition is a statement that is either true or false; logical connectives combine propositions into compound statements.

examples.solution: Use base case and inductive step.
Apply De Morgan and distributive laws.

notes: State base case clearly for induction

theory: Set theory and functions form the foundation of data model descriptions and type systems used in computing.
Equivalence relations partition sets and support grouping concepts like hashing and canonical forms.
Understanding injections, surjections, and bijections helps when reasoning about reversibility of mappings in programs.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: A function f from A to B assigns each element of A to exactly one element of B; relations are subsets of Cartesian products.

keyPoints: Use in database keys and mappings

theory: Counting principles estimate solution spaces and are used in complexity estimates and enumeration problems.
Basic graph definitions introduce vertices, edges, paths and cycles—the language for modeling networks and dependencies.
Combinatorial reasoning underpins probabilistic analysis and hashing methods in IT systems.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: A graph G=(V,E) consists of a vertex set V and an edge set E connecting pairs of vertices.

theory: Fourier analysis decomposes signals into frequency components used in signal processing and compression algorithms.
Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) enables frequency-domain processing on sampled data and underlies FFT implementations used widely in engineering.
Understanding aliasing and sampling interplay is essential when digitizing analog signals for processing in IT systems.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Fourier transform converts a time-domain signal into its frequency-domain representation.

notes: Use libraries for FFT in practice

theory: Sampling theory prescribes conditions to reconstruct analog signals from samples and avoid information loss.
Anti-aliasing filters prevent high-frequency content from folding into the sampled band when digitizing signals.
Interpolation methods reconstruct continuous approximations and are used in resampling and display scaling.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Nyquist theorem states that to reconstruct a band-limited signal, sampling frequency must exceed twice the maximum signal frequency.

theory: Z-transform provides a tool for analyzing discrete-time systems, their stability and response—useful in digital filters and control loops.
Pole-zero analysis aids in designing stable filters and understanding resonance in discrete systems.
Difference equations model recurrence relations in algorithms and digital signal processes.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Z-transform X(z)=Σ x[n] z^{−n} converts a discrete sequence into a complex function for analysis.

keyPoints: Relate to DFT for frequency analysis
notes: Map z-plane to frequency for interpretation

theory: Breadth-First and Depth-First Search are fundamental traversals used for exploring graphs, finding shortest paths in unweighted graphs, and topological ordering.
Traversal methods underpin many algorithms for connectivity, component labeling, and exploring state spaces in computation problems.
Efficient implementation uses adjacency lists and avoids repeated visits via marking arrays.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: BFS explores vertices in layers from a source, while DFS follows paths deeply before backtracking.

keyPoints: BFS uses queue, DFS uses stack/recursion
BFS finds shortest path in unweighted graphs
Track visited nodes to avoid cycles
Use adjacency lists for sparse graphs
Applications include web crawling and AI search

examples.solution: Track recursion stack to find back edges.

notes: Specify starting node and graph type

examQuestions.question: Use BFS to find shortest path.

theory: Shortest path algorithms compute minimal-cost routes; Dijkstra solves non-negative weighted graphs efficiently with priority queues.
Minimum spanning trees connect all nodes with minimal total weight and find use in network design and clustering.
Union-Find data structure accelerates Kruskal's algorithm for MST construction.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: A minimum spanning tree is a subset of edges connecting all vertices with minimal total weight.

keyPoints: Choose correct algorithm for graph density
notes: Check for negative edges before Dijkstra

examQuestions.question: Show one step of Prim's algorithm.

theory: Recurrence relations express runtime of recursive algorithms; solving them gives asymptotic bounds used to compare algorithm efficiency.
Amortized analysis distributes occasional expensive operations across many cheap ones to reason about average cost per operation.
Master theorem provides quick solutions to many divide-and-conquer recurrences encountered in algorithm design.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: A recurrence relation defines a function in terms of its values on smaller inputs, often arising from recursive algorithms.

keyPoints: Apply Master theorem when forms match

examples.solution: O(n log n) via Master theorem.

theory: Practical exercises reinforce theory by applying traversal and shortest-path algorithms to network routing and small datasets.
Modeling real networks involves mapping nodes and edges with weights representing latency, bandwidth or cost.
Small projects illustrate the role of graph algorithms in search engines, social networks and routing protocols.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Routing selects paths in networks to deliver messages along minimal-cost or policy-compliant routes.

examples.problem: Model a small network and run Dijkstra
examples.solution: Assign link weights and compute paths.

notes: Keep datasets small for lab timing

theory: Project work applies learned discrete and algorithmic concepts to small-team problems.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Project demonstrates integration of theory and implementation.

examples.solution: Code and test with sample graphs.

theory: A revision unit consolidates knowledge and practices with mixed problems and past-year questions.

definition.label: Definition
definition.text: Revision focuses on problem-solving across topics to prepare for assessments.

examples.solution: Apply appropriate methods from each unit.

notes: Create answer templates for common problems
references: Problem Solving in Discrete Math - Author
